md: Here's a trick that looks like magic. Take any number, say $\,738$. Add its digits: $7 + 3 + 8 = 18$. Since $18$ is divisible by $9$, so is $738$. (Check: $738 = 9 \times 82$.)
It works every time, for $3$ as well as $9$. Try a few numbers in the explorer, then read why it can't fail.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: A whole number is divisible by $9$ exactly when its **digit sum** is divisible by $9$, and the same holds for $3$.

md: ### The one fact that powers everything
Look at the building blocks of place value ($10$, $100$, $1000$) and notice each is just **one more than a string of 9s**:
And $9$, $99$, $999$ are all divisible by $9$ (and by $3$). That's the whole secret.
Write a number using place value. Take $738$:
Now swap each power of $10$ for its "$9$s plus $1$" form:
Multiply out and **sort the pieces into two piles**, the parts with $99$ or $9$ in them, and the leftover $+1$s:
The first pile is divisible by $9$ no matter what the digits are, it's built entirely from $9$s. So whether the **whole number** is divisible by $9$ depends *only* on the second pile: the digit sum.
If the digit sum is a multiple of $9$, then $738$ = (multiple of $9$) + (multiple of $9$) = a multiple of $9$. If it isn't, the leftover stops the whole number from being divisible. That's the test.

blocks.title: Why 3 comes along for free
blocks.body: Every number in the first pile ($9$, $99$, $999$) is also divisible by $3$. So the identical argument works with $3$ in place of $9$. That's why the digit-sum test handles **both** divisors at once. (It does *not* work for $7$, because $10$ is not $7+1$.)
